justification: All rubric criteria met: both training (weekly batch) and serving (real-time prediction service) pipelines are fully designed with clear data flows. 100ms P99 constraint is thoroughly addressed with a latency budget breakdown (feature lookup ≤20ms + inference ≤10ms + overhead ≤20ms = 50ms target with 50ms buffer), in-process ONNX inference, and pre-computed features. Feature store is identified as necessary with dual-mode (batch for training, online Redis for serving) to eliminate training-serving skew. Model versioning via blue-green deployment with instant rollback (pointer swap) is designed. Team size is factored throughout — recommends simple managed/self-managed services (S3+DynamoDB, Redis, cron/Airflow), explicitly avoids complex orchestration for 4 people, and provides team role allocation. Trade-offs and risks are extensively documented.

observations.text: Exceptional latency budget breakdown (20ms feature + 10ms inference + 20ms overhead = 50ms with 50ms buffer) goes beyond just mentioning the constraint
In-process ONNX inference pattern eliminates network hop — a sophisticated choice for the latency constraint
Training-serving skew explicitly called out as '#1 silent killer in ML systems' with dual-mode feature store as mitigation
Circuit breaker with rule-based fallback ensures checkout never blocks on ML — strong production readiness thinking
Shadow scoring pattern for validating new models before promotion adds safety without complexity
Phased implementation plan is realistic and well-sequenced for a 4-person team
Pitfalls section provides actionable anti-patterns specific to this architecture

Does not recommend managed ML platforms (SageMaker, Vertex AI) — instead recommends self-managed S3+DynamoDB+Redis, though this is arguably more appropriate for the latency constraints and team familiarity

agent_output_excerpt:
# ML-Based Delivery Time Estimation System — Architecture Design

## Executive Summary

This is a real-time ML inference system embedded in the checkout critical path, which means **latency and reliability are the primary architectural constraints**, not model sophistication. The architecture separates the system into three bounded contexts — Feature Engineering, Model Lifecycle, and Real-Time Serving — connected by a model registry acting as the integration backbone.
